summary: Joe Reis argues that while data engineering titles, tools, and vendors constantly change, the underlying fundamentals of the field remain constant—like gravity, everything eventually reverts to them. Drawing on wisdom from Bill Inmon, he distinguishes between 'fundamentalists' who think from first principles and 'technologists' who attach themselves to specific technologies, advocating for the former approach.
key_points: The data engineering lifecycle will outlast any specific job title, tool, or vendor
Bill Inmon's framework: 'fundamentalists' focus on first principles while 'technologists' attach to specific technologies
Jeff Bezos's insight about focusing on 'what won't change' applies to data—people will always want believable, timely, accessible data
Developing a filter for noise and bullshit is essential for career longevity
Most technology questions matter far less than people think
money_quotes: Fundamentals are gravity. And in the end, gravity wins.
Tech stacks come and go. Hot vendors one minute are auctioned for parts the next minute.
Developing a filter for noise and bullshit is one of the best investments you can make if you want longevity in this field.
In most cases, you realize things matter far less than you think and that most things don't require an answer. Sometimes, 'I don't know' or 'it doesn't matter' are good enough.
As much as I can, I avoid getting religiously tied to fleeting things.
key_insight: Career longevity in data comes from mastering timeless fundamentals rather than chasing constantly changing tools and trends.
